title: Turbulence at 30,000 Feet
briefing: Three hours into a transcontinental flight, tech founder Elliot Pryce collapsed after a sip of his in-flight drink -- poisoned, and now recovering in a hospital on the ground below. The captain diverted, the cabin was sealed, and five people in business class are the only ones who could have reached his glass.

suspects.name: Priya Sethi
suspects.persona: Brisk and efficient, mildly impatient about the disruption to her schedule. Warms up noticeably when the conversation turns to her actual work.

timeline.text: I boarded with everyone else, took my seat in 2B, and spent most of the flight finishing a deck on my laptop.

motive.text: I'd never even heard of Elliot Pryce before a flight attendant pointed him out after the fact. I have no history with the man at all.

relationships.text: I exchanged maybe ten words with him the entire flight, mostly complaints about the wifi.

alibi.text: I was at my seat working right up until the commotion started. The woman across the aisle was awake too, she can probably confirm that.

evidenceReactions.text: ...I did step into the galley once, to ask for more coffee. I was there maybe thirty seconds. I never went anywhere near his row.

backgroundCheck.text: Clean record. Verified consulting employment, no documented connection to Pryce or his company.

initialLie.text: I was in my seat with my laptop the entire flight, I never once got up.

suspects.name: Marcus Yeun
suspects.persona: Professional and clipped, protective of cabin procedure, visibly anxious about liability and his certification.

timeline.text: Drink service ran normally through business class between the first and second hour. Nothing unusual until Mr. Pryce's collapse.

motive.text: I don't have any stake in this beyond keeping my cabin safe. An incident like this is a nightmare for my certification, not something I'd invite.

relationships.text: I served him his drink the same as anyone else in that cabin. No relationship beyond that.

alibi.text: I was working the cabin for the entire flight, same as any other shift.

evidenceReactions.text: ...I stepped away for about two minutes to help a colleague with a passenger in economy. The cart was unattended in the aisle for that window. I should have locked it down first. That's on me.

backgroundCheck.text: Eight years of service, clean record, certification current.

initialLie.text: I personally poured and served every drink in that cabin myself, the cart was never out of my sight.

suspects.name: Diane Cho
suspects.persona: Sharp and observant, a little imperious, clearly enjoys being the one who notices things -- but guarded about her own reasons for traveling.

timeline.text: I was seated across the aisle from Mr. Pryce, in 2D. Quiet flight, right up until the commotion.

motive.text: I'd never laid eyes on the man before this flight. Whatever happened to him is none of my doing.

relationships.text: We exchanged pleasantries when he sat down. Nothing more than that.

alibi.text: I dozed on and off for most of the flight. It was a long one.

evidenceReactions.text: ...Alright, I woke up once to ask for a blanket. I was awake for a few minutes around then. I didn't see anything unusual, or I'd have said so already.

backgroundCheck.text: No criminal history. One unrelated note: barred from this airline's lounge network three years ago after a dispute over a seating upgrade.

initialLie.text: I was asleep for the entire flight, I didn't see or hear a thing.

suspects.name: Felix Adeyemi
suspects.persona: Energetic and a little combative, quick to justify himself, clearly still bitter about a public dispute he'd rather not relitigate.

timeline.text: I was seated a few rows back in 4A, nowhere near him for most of the flight.

motive.text: Fine, yes, Elliot and I have history. He copied a feature from my product and out-marketed me for it. I've said plenty about that publicly, but I didn't put anything in his drink.

relationships.text: We've clashed online for over a year. I genuinely didn't expect to be on the same flight as him.

alibi.text: I stayed in my seat except to use the bathroom once, well before he collapsed.

evidenceReactions.text: ...We had words at the gate, yes. He made a comment, I made one back. It was ugly for about thirty seconds, and then we both got on the plane and sat as far apart as business class allows.

backgroundCheck.text: No criminal record. Public dispute with Pryce's company is well documented, including a cease-and-desist exchange last year.

initialLie.text: I hadn't spoken to Elliot at all today, we didn't even acknowledge each other.

suspects.name: Renee Castillo
suspects.persona: Composed and pleasant, gives short, slightly rehearsed-sounding answers. Becomes noticeably more careful and clipped whenever the conversation drifts toward Elliot Pryce specifically.

timeline.text: I was seated in 2A, by the window. I kept to myself with a book for most of the flight.

motive.text: I have a career and a family that have nothing to do with whatever industry drama Mr. Pryce is tangled up in. I'm honestly not sure why you'd look at me.

relationships.text: I didn't know him. I recognized his name from the news, same as anyone would.

alibi.text: I didn't leave my seat except once, early on, before drink service even started.

evidenceReactions.text: ...That account is old. I used my maiden name for years before I got married. Elliot and I crossed paths a long time ago, in a different life. I didn't think it was relevant to mention.

backgroundCheck.text: Frequent-flyer account originally registered years ago under the name Renee Ashford, with a professional background in pharmaceutical chemistry. Several historical itineraries overlap with Elliot Pryce's during that period.

initialLie.text: I'd never met Elliot Pryce before today, I only know him from the headlines like everyone else.

evidence.label: Galley service log
evidence.description: The galley log shows Priya Sethi briefly entered the galley near the drink cart for under a minute, requesting coffee.

evidence.label: Crew handoff log
evidence.description: Marcus Yeun briefly left the cabin to assist another crew member, leaving the drink cart unattended in the aisle for roughly two minutes.

evidence.label: Call button log
evidence.description: Diane Cho's call button was used once mid-flight, requesting a blanket, during the window she claimed to have been fully asleep.

evidence.label: Gate agent's note
evidence.description: A gate agent documented a brief but heated exchange between Felix Adeyemi and Elliot Pryce during boarding.

evidence.label: Frequent-flyer account history
evidence.description: Renee Castillo's frequent-flyer account was originally registered years ago under the name Renee Ashford, with several historical itineraries overlapping Elliot Pryce's during that period.

timeline.text: Boarding begins; a gate agent notes a brief, tense exchange near the jet bridge.
Elliot Pryce is served his drink during the first cabin service pass.
Priya Sethi says she never left her seat the entire flight.
Marcus Yeun says he personally served every drink and never left the cart unattended.
Diane Cho says she was asleep for most of the flight.
Felix Adeyemi says he hadn't spoken to Elliot Pryce at all that day.
Renee Castillo says she'd never met Elliot Pryce before today.
Elliot Pryce complains of dizziness and collapses in his seat.
The crew requests medical volunteers and the captain begins preparations to divert.
Flight 914 diverts and lands; paramedics take Elliot Pryce to a hospital, stable but seriously ill.

solution.narrative:
Renee Castillo did know Elliot Pryce -- intimately, once. Years ago, as Renee Ashford, she co-founded Elliot's first company with him, both professionally and personally engaged to be married. When the company found real traction, Elliot maneuvered her out of the business in a boardroom vote she never saw coming, rewriting the founding story to erase her contribution entirely -- and the engagement collapsed in the same season, for the same reasons. Renee rebuilt quietly under her married name, retrained as a pharmaceutical chemist, and never stopped tracking his career from a distance.

Booking herself onto his flight was not a coincidence. Seated one row behind him in business class, she watched the cabin service pass and waited for a moment the aisle was clear -- Marcus's brief absence from the cart gave her exactly the gap she needed. Using a compound from her professional background, easy for her to obtain and easy to dose without a trace of flavor, she altered his drink during that short window and returned to her seat with a book, precisely as she'd described to everyone who asked.

It wasn't rage or a public scene. It was years of quiet planning, a stolen professional skill turned into a weapon, and a name change that meant nobody on that plane thought to ask the one question that mattered: had she ever met him before?
